meta.name: Kernel Bypass Network Stack
meta.description: Build a user-space TCP/IP stack using kernel bypass techniques (DPDK or AF_XDP) for ultra-low latency packet processing in HFT environments.
meta.essence: Direct hardware access via kernel bypass, user-space network stack implementation (TCP, UDP, IP, ARP), zero-copy packet processing, and poll-mode drivers for minimal latency in the microsecond range.
meta.why_important: This is the holy grail of HFT infrastructure. Engineers who can build kernel bypass network stacks earn $400K-1M+ at top trading firms. This skill set is extremely rare and highly valued.
learning_outcomes: Understand kernel bypass techniques (DPDK, AF_XDP, io_uring)
Implement user-space TCP/IP stack from scratch
Handle ARP, IP, TCP, UDP protocols
Debug network stack issues at packet level

prerequisites.name: Understanding of Ethernet, IP, TCP protocols

milestones.name: Kernel Bypass Setup
milestones.description: Set up kernel bypass infrastructure using DPDK or AF_XDP for direct packet access from user space.
acceptance_criteria: DPDK or AF_XDP initialized and working
Network interface bound to user-space driver
Packet reception in user space without kernel involvement
pitfalls: DPDK requires hugepages and specific kernel config
AF_XDP requires newer kernel (4.18+)
NIC binding removes interface from kernel networking
Driver compatibility varies by NIC vendor
Debugging without kernel tools is difficult

milestones.name: Ethernet & ARP Layer
milestones.description: Implement Ethernet frame processing and ARP protocol for link-layer communication.
acceptance_criteria: ARP cache for MAC address lookup
pitfalls: ARP cache must handle timeouts and invalidation
Ethernet padding for minimum frame size
MAC address byte order differs by architecture
Promiscuous mode needed for all frames
ARP spoofing security considerations

milestones.name: IP & UDP Layer
milestones.description: Implement IPv4 processing and UDP protocol for datagram communication.
pitfalls: IP fragmentation kills performance; avoid with MSS
Checksum offload not available in user space
IP options rarely used but must handle
Fragment reassembly DoS attacks
TTL handling and ICMP responses

milestones.name: TCP Implementation
milestones.description: Implement a minimal but functional TCP stack with connection management, reliable delivery, and congestion control.
acceptance_criteria: Support for at least 10K concurrent connections
pitfalls: TCP state machine is complex; many edge cases
Retransmission timeout calculation critical
Sequence number wraparound (PAWS)
SYN flood attacks; use SYN cookies
Zero-window probes for stuck receivers

milestones.name: Optimization & Production Hardening
milestones.description: Optimize the stack for ultra-low latency and add production features like monitoring, debugging, and failover.
pitfalls: Locks in data path destroy latency
Cross-NUMA memory access is slow
Batch processing increases latency
Statistics collection adds overhead
Production NICs behave differently from lab

blueprint.title: Kernel Bypass Network Stack
blueprint.overview:
Build a complete user-space TCP/IP stack using kernel bypass techniques (DPDK or AF_XDP) for ultra-low latency packet processing. This project strips away the kernel's network abstraction layer, giving your code direct access to NIC hardware—a technique used in high-frequency trading where microseconds translate directly to profit or loss. You'll implement every protocol layer from Ethernet frames through TCP congestion control, all while maintaining zero-copy data paths and sub-microsecond latencies.

The project forces confrontation with fundamental networking concepts that remain invisible when using kernel sockets. How does ARP actually resolve MAC addresses? What happens when an IP packet exceeds the MTU? Why does TCP need a state machine with eleven states? By building each layer from scratch, you'll understand network protocols at the level needed to debug production failures and optimize for extreme performance.

This is the networking equivalent of building your own memory allocator—you may never need to do it in production, but understanding the internals transforms how you approach every system that depends on networking.
blueprint.design_philosophy:
This project teaches through the 'removal of abstraction.' Standard network programming uses kernel sockets that hide 40+ years of protocol evolution behind a simple API. Kernel bypass forces you to implement every detail yourself. The learning comes not from memorizing RFCs, but from understanding why each protocol component exists and how it fails.

We structure the project in layers that mirror the actual protocol stack, but with a twist: each milestone includes a 'hardware soul' analysis showing cache behavior, memory access patterns, and latency implications. This isn't just about correctness—it's about building something that processes packets in under 2 microseconds.

The final milestone on optimization isn't an afterthought; it's where the real engineering happens. A correct TCP implementation that takes 50 microseconds per packet is useless for HFT. We teach optimization as a first-class concern, not a premature afterthought.

implementation.rationale: Kernel bypass networking requires direct hardware access, manual memory management, and precise control over data layout. DPDK's primary API is C, and AF_XDP uses C structures directly. C provides zero-cost abstractions, explicit memory ordering control via barriers, and direct pointer manipulation for zero-copy buffer management. Rust is viable but adds complexity for the raw memory operations needed in packet processing.
implementation.style_guide: C11 standard. Snake_case for functions/variables. UPPERCASE for macros/constants. All structs packed where protocol layout requires. Explicit endianness conversion using ntohl/htonl family. Memory barriers documented with comments. No dynamic allocation in data path—all buffers pre-allocated in pools.
implementation.build_system: Makefile with separate targets for DPDK and AF_XDP backends. pkg-config for DPDK libraries. Debug builds with -O0 -g, release with -O3 -march=native -flto.
alternatives: Rust — Memory safety benefits, but DPDK bindings are less mature and raw pointer manipulation requires unsafe blocks throughout
C++ — Zero-cost abstractions with nicer generics, but vtables and exceptions have hidden costs; most HFT firms use pure C for critical paths

assumed_known: C pointers, structs, and memory layout
What MAC and IP addresses are

must_teach_first.why: Understanding syscall overhead explains why kernel bypass matters
Packet reception is fundamentally about DMA, not software
User-space drivers communicate via MMIO, not function calls
Every protocol field requires correct byte order handling
Flow control is impossible to implement without understanding window mechanics

milestones.title: Kernel Bypass Setup
milestones.summary: Establish direct NIC access from user space using DPDK or AF_XDP, bypassing the kernel network stack entirely. Set up zero-copy buffer pools and achieve packet RX/TX without a single syscall in the data path.
milestones.misconception: Developers think the kernel is 'fast enough' for networking—after all, web servers handle millions of requests per second. They assume the kernel's TCP/IP stack is a black box that can't be improved upon.
milestones.reveal: The kernel network stack adds 10-50 microseconds of latency per packet through context switches, sk_buff allocations, protocol processing, and queueing. Kernel bypass eliminates ALL of this—you get packets directly from NIC DMA before the kernel even knows they exist. The difference isn't incremental; it's 10-100x faster.
cascade: Memory-mapped I/O for GPU drivers — Same technique of mapping device registers into user space for direct hardware control
io_uring for storage — Another kernel bypass technique; the pattern of avoiding syscalls applies universally
DMA engines in embedded systems — Understanding NIC DMA transfers directly applies to any device mastering the bus
Lock-free ring buffers — The RX/TX queues you build here are the same pattern used in audio processing, game loops, and real-time systems
NUMA-aware allocation for databases — The memory locality concerns in packet buffers are identical to column-store page allocation
yaml_acceptance_criteria: DPDK or AF_XDP initialized and working
Network interface bound to user-space driver
Packet reception in user space without kernel involvement

milestones.title: Ethernet & ARP Layer
milestones.summary: Implement link-layer processing: parse Ethernet frames, handle MAC addressing, and build a functional ARP implementation for IP-to-MAC resolution. This is the foundation all higher protocols depend on.
milestones.misconception: Developers think Ethernet is trivial—just a 14-byte header with MAC addresses. They've never considered what happens when they send a packet to an IP address: how does the NIC know which physical device to send to?
milestones.reveal: Ethernet is the only protocol that actually touches the wire. IP addresses are purely logical abstractions; the physical network only understands MAC addresses. ARP is the bridge between these worlds, and without a working ARP cache, no communication is possible. Your TCP stack is useless if ARP fails.
cascade: Spanning Tree Protocol in switches — Understanding MAC learning and broadcast domains explains why ARP requests flood the network
IPv6 Neighbor Discovery — ARP's successor uses the same fundamental concept of address resolution
MAC address tables in switches — The same learning algorithm switches use is what your ARP cache implements
Load balancer MAC-based routing — L2 load balancing relies on the same MAC manipulation you're learning
VPN tunnel endpoints — GRE/VXLAN encapsulation is just Ethernet-in-Ethernet with the same parsing logic
yaml_acceptance_criteria: ARP cache for MAC address lookup

milestones.title: IP & UDP Layer
milestones.summary: Build the network layer with IPv4 processing, fragmentation handling, and UDP datagram support. Implement ICMP for reachability testing. This milestone bridges link-layer communication to transport-layer services.
milestones.misconception: Developers think IP fragmentation is a solved problem—they've never seen it because modern networks avoid it with Path MTU Discovery. They assume UDP is just 'TCP without the guarantees' and don't understand its role.
milestones.reveal: IP fragmentation is a performance catastrophe that can reduce throughput by 90% or more. Every fragment must be reassembled, consuming memory and CPU, and a single lost fragment destroys the entire packet. UDP's value isn't simplicity—it's control. You decide when to send, how much, and whether to wait. In HFT, that control is worth the reliability trade-off.
cascade: IPsec VPN implementation — IP-in-IP encapsulation uses the same header parsing and TTL management
DNS server implementation — UDP is the primary DNS transport; you now understand why
QUIC protocol — HTTP/3's UDP foundation makes sense when you see how much control UDP provides
Load balancer health checks — ICMP echo implementation directly applies to service monitoring
Database sharding routers — IP-based routing decisions require the same header inspection you're building

milestones.title: TCP Implementation
milestones.summary: Implement a complete TCP state machine with connection management, reliable delivery via retransmission, sliding window flow control, and congestion control. This is the most complex milestone, requiring careful state management and timer handling.
milestones.misconception: Developers think TCP is just 'reliable UDP'—send data and it arrives. They don't understand the eleven-state machine, the timing complexity of retransmission, or why congestion control exists at all.
milestones.reveal: TCP is arguably the most complex widely-deployed protocol in existence. It must handle packets arriving out of order, duplicated, delayed by seconds, or lost entirely—all while providing a simple byte-stream abstraction to applications. The congestion control algorithms (Reno, CUBIC, BBR) represent decades of research into how to share network capacity fairly without central coordination.
cascade: QUIC's TCP replacement — Understanding TCP's weaknesses (head-of-line blocking, handshake latency) explains why QUIC exists
Database connection pooling — TCP connection establishment cost is why pools exist; you'll see the 3-way handshake overhead firsthand
Message queue reliability — Kafka's exactly-once semantics build on the same acknowledgment concepts as TCP
HTTP/2 multiplexing — The stream management is simplified TCP, trading complexity for parallelism
Distributed consensus (Raft/Paxos) — Leader election and log replication use concepts directly analogous to TCP sequence numbers and acknowledgments
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Support for at least 10K concurrent connections

milestones.title: Optimization & Production Hardening
milestones.summary: Transform a working stack into a production-ready system. Achieve sub-5-microsecond latency through NUMA-aware allocation, lock-free data paths, batch processing, and careful cache optimization. Add monitoring, debugging tools, and deployment documentation.
milestones.misconception: Developers think optimization is premature optimization—'make it work, then make it fast.' They believe modern hardware is fast enough that micro-optimizations don't matter, and that clean code is more important than cache locality.
milestones.reveal: In HFT, a single cache miss costing 100 nanoseconds can mean the difference between profiting from a price move and arriving too late. The difference between a 5μs and 10μs stack isn't 5μs—it's the difference between being first to market or being arbitraged away. Optimization at this level isn't premature; it's the entire point.
cascade: Game engine render loops — The same cache-line awareness and lock-free design applies to 60fps rendering
Audio processing pipelines — Real-time audio has the same latency constraints; buffer underruns are as fatal as dropped packets
Database query execution — The vectorization and batch processing techniques apply to column-store scanning
High-frequency signal processing — FPGA-to-CPU DMA patterns are identical to NIC-to-CPU patterns
Compiler optimization passes — Understanding cache behavior helps you write code the optimizer can't improve

diagrams.title: Kernel Bypass Network Stack — Satellite View
diagrams.description: System-wide map showing all five milestones and their interconnections. Shows the layered architecture from NIC hardware through application API, with optimization concerns spanning all layers. This is the home base for the entire project.

diagrams.title: Kernel Path vs. Bypass Path — Latency Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of packet journey through kernel network stack (interrupt → softirq → sk_buff allocation → protocol layers → socket buffer → copy to user) versus bypass path (NIC DMA directly to user buffer → poll detection → immediate processing). Shows where microseconds are lost.

diagrams.title: NIC Ring Buffer Architecture
diagrams.description: Memory layout showing RX and TX descriptor rings, buffer pointers, DMA engine, and NIC registers. Shows how head/tail pointers enable zero-copy operation and where memory barriers are required.

diagrams.title: DPDK vs. AF_XDP Architecture
diagrams.description: Comparison of the two main kernel bypass approaches. DPDK uses Poll Mode Drivers (PMDs) with full NIC control; AF_XDP uses kernel-managed XDP programs with user-space queues. Shows trade-offs in flexibility, kernel version requirements, and performance characteristics.

diagrams.title: Packet Reception — Step-by-Step Trace
diagrams.description: Timeline of packet arrival: wire → PHY → MAC → DMA → RX ring update → poll detection → buffer ownership transfer. Shows timing at each stage with typical nanosecond values.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Buffer Pool Architecture
diagrams.description: Structure of the buffer pool: fixed-size buffers, free list management, reference counting for buffer sharing, and NUMA-aware allocation. Shows how buffers flow from free → RX → processing → TX → free.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool Cache Line Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of buffer pool structures showing cache line boundaries at 64 bytes. Identifies hot fields (refcount, next pointer) vs. cold fields (debug info) and potential false sharing issues.

diagrams.title: Latency Measurement Methodology
diagrams.description: Hardware setup for sub-microsecond latency measurement: timestamping at NIC hardware level (PTP), FPGA-based packet generator, and the statistical methodology (percentiles, outliers) for meaningful latency claims.

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Structure — Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Complete Ethernet frame showing preamble, SFD, destination MAC, source MAC, EtherType, payload, FCS. Byte offsets marked. Minimum/maximum frame sizes. VLAN tag insertion point. Cache line alignment considerations.

diagrams.title: ARP Resolution State Machine
diagrams.description: States: IDLE → REQUESTING → RESOLVED → STALE → FAILED. Transitions triggered by timeouts, responses, and cache lookups. Retry counters and exponential backoff.

diagrams.title: ARP Cache — Hash Table with LRU
diagrams.description: Data structure for ARP cache: hash table indexed by IP address, each bucket containing MAC, timestamp, state, and LRU links. Shows collision handling and eviction policy.

diagrams.title: ARP Request/Reply Packet Flow
diagrams.description: Network-level diagram showing broadcast ARP request, unicast reply, and cache population on both sides. Includes gratuitous ARP for duplicate address detection.

diagrams.title: MAC Address Byte Order Confusion
diagrams.description: How the same MAC address appears in memory on little-endian vs. big-endian systems. Shows wire order vs. host order and the conversion functions needed.

diagrams.title: IPv4 Header — Complete Byte Layout
diagrams.description: All 20-60 bytes of IPv4 header with field positions, bit boundaries, and alignment. Version, IHL, TOS/DSCP, total length, identification, flags, fragment offset, TTL, protocol, checksum, addresses. Options handling.

diagrams.title: IP Fragmentation and Reassembly
diagrams.description: How a large IP packet is fragmented for MTU, fragment headers with offsets and flags, reassembly timer and buffer management, and why this destroys performance.

diagrams.title: IP/UDP/TCP Checksum — One's Complement
diagrams.description: The internet checksum algorithm: one's complement sum, folding 32-bit to 16-bit, incremental update optimization for header changes. Shows SIMD vectorization opportunity.

diagrams.title: UDP Datagram Processing Pipeline
diagrams.description: UDP datagram from IP layer delivery to socket queue: port demultiplexing, checksum verification, socket buffer enqueue. Shows the minimal processing that makes UDP fast.

diagrams.title: ICMP Message Types and Layout
diagrams.description: ICMP header structure, common message types (echo request/reply, destination unreachable, time exceeded), and how each is processed.

diagrams.title: IP Routing Table — Longest Prefix Match
diagrams.description: Routing table structure with prefix/mask pairs, longest prefix match algorithm (LPM trie or hash), and interface selection. Shows multi-homed host scenario.

diagrams.title: TCP State Machine — Complete
diagrams.description: All eleven TCP states (CLOSED, LISTEN, SYN_SENT, SYN_RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN_WAIT_1, FIN_WAIT_2, CLOSING, TIME_WAIT, CLOSE_WAIT, LAST_ACK) with transitions triggered by SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK, FIN, RST, and timeouts.

diagrams.title: TCP Header — Complete Byte Layout
diagrams.description: All TCP header fields with bit positions: src/dst port, seq number, ack number, data offset, reserved, flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN), window, checksum, urgent pointer. Option layout (MSS, window scale, SACK, timestamp).

diagrams.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake — Packet Exchange
diagrams.description: Timeline of SYN → SYN+ACK → ACK exchange showing sequence number initialization, option negotiation (MSS, window scale, SACK), and state transitions on both sides.

diagrams.title: TCP Connection Teardown — FIN/RST Paths
diagrams.description: Normal close: FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK with TIME_WAIT explanation. Abortive close with RST. Simultaneous close scenario.

diagrams.title: TCP Sliding Window — Visual Mechanics
diagrams.description: Byte stream with send window, receive window, and their positions relative to sequence numbers. Shows how window advertisements control flow and the relationship between window size and bandwidth-delay product.

diagrams.title: TCP Retransmission Timer — RTO Calculation
diagrams.description: How RTO is computed from RTT samples using Jacobson/Karels algorithm: SRTT, RTTVAR, and the exponential backoff on retransmission. Shows why accurate RTT measurement is critical.

diagrams.title: TCP Reno Congestion Control — AIMD
diagrams.description: Congestion window growth in slow start (exponential) and congestion avoidance (linear), loss detection via triple duplicate ACK vs. timeout, and multiplicative decrease. CWND and SSTHRESH state.

diagrams.title: Connection Table — 10K Connections Design
diagrams.description: Data structure for tracking 10K+ connections: hash table keyed by (src IP, src port, dst IP, dst port), connection entry layout, and lock-free lookup using RCU or hazard pointers.

diagrams.title: SYN Cookies — SYN Flood Defense
diagrams.description: How SYN cookies encode connection state into the ISN, allowing SYN_RECEIVED state to be stateless. Trade-off: loss of options negotiation.

diagrams.title: TCP Sequence Number Wraparound (PAWS)
diagrams.description: How 32-bit sequence numbers wrap every 4GB, why timestamps (PAWS) are needed to distinguish old from new segments, and the arithmetic for comparison across the wrap boundary.

diagrams.title: NUMA Topology for Packet Processing
diagrams.description: Multi-socket system with NIC on specific NUMA node, memory allocation on correct node, and the latency penalty for cross-NUMA access. Shows optimal thread placement.

diagrams.title: Lock-Free Ring Buffer for Packet Queues
diagrams.description: Single-producer single-consumer ring buffer using head/tail indices with memory ordering. Shows acquire/release semantics and why this is faster than mutex-protected queues.

diagrams.title: Batch Size vs. Latency Trade-off
diagrams.description: Graph showing throughput vs. latency for different batch sizes. Small batches = low latency but high per-packet overhead. Large batches = high throughput but increased tail latency. Finding the sweet spot.

diagrams.title: Packet Processing Cache Locality
diagrams.description: Memory access pattern during packet processing: which cache lines are touched, prefetching opportunities, and struct layout optimizations (hot fields first).

diagrams.title: Statistics Collection — Lock-Free Counters
diagrams.description: Per-thread counters with atomic increments or thread-local accumulation, aggregation for reporting, and minimizing the overhead of observability.

diagrams.title: In-Stack Packet Capture for Debugging
diagrams.description: How to implement wireshark-compatible packet capture without impacting data path: mirror port, ring buffer capture, and pcap file format.

diagrams.title: Production Deployment Architecture
diagrams.description: Complete production setup: NIC bonding for redundancy, heartbeat for failover, management interface separate from data interface, and integration with trading systems.

diagrams.title: Latency Distribution — Why P99 Matters More Than Mean
diagrams.description: Histogram of packet processing latencies showing typical bi-modal distribution (fast path vs. slow path), why mean latency is misleading, and the importance of tail latency in HFT.

diagrams.title: Complete Stack — All Layers Integrated
diagrams.description: Full vertical slice from application API through TCP → IP → Ethernet → NIC driver → DMA. Shows buffer ownership transfer at each layer and where copies are avoided.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Data Path — Buffer Lifetime
diagrams.description: Complete lifecycle of a buffer: allocation from pool → NIC DMA write → stack processing → application read → reuse. Shows where zero-copy is maintained and where copies might be necessary.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A complete user-space TCP/IP stack using kernel bypass (DPDK/AF_XDP) for ultra-low latency packet processing in HFT environments. The stack eliminates kernel overhead by directly accessing NIC hardware via DMA, implements all protocol layers from Ethernet through TCP with congestion control, and achieves sub-5μs packet-to-application latency through cache-optimized, lock-free data paths on NUMA-aware memory allocations.

modules.name: Kernel Bypass Setup
modules.description: Establishes direct NIC access via DPDK or AF_XDP, bypassing the kernel network stack. Manages zero-copy packet buffer pools, ring buffer initialization, and poll-mode driver configuration. Does NOT handle any protocol parsing—that's delegated to subsequent layers.

specs.inputs: NIC PCI address or interface name, hugepage configuration, NUMA node selection
specs.outputs: Initialized packet RX/TX rings, allocated UMEM/mbuf pool, functional packet reception and transmission
specs.abstractions: UMEM (User Memory) region for AF_XDP, rte_mbuf pool for DPDK, ring buffer descriptor queues (fill/RX/TX/completion), memory-mapped I/O for NIC registers
error_categories: E_NIC_INIT_FAILED - NIC initialization failure (driver incompatibility, firmware issues)
E_HUGEPAGE_EXHAUSTED - Insufficient hugepages for buffer allocation
E_DMA_MAPPING_FAILED - DMA mapping failure (IOMMU issues, physical address constraints)
E_RING_FULL - TX ring full (backpressure from NIC)
E_RING_EMPTY - RX ring empty (no packets available)
E_UMEM_ALLOC_FAILED - UMEM/mbuf pool allocation failure
E_NUMA_MISMATCH - Memory allocated on wrong NUMA node
E_PERMISSION_DENIED - Insufficient privileges for device access
specs.concurrency_model: Single-producer single-consumer (SPSC) lock-free ring buffers per queue. Per-core buffer cache in DPDK. No locks in data path. Memory barriers for producer/consumer synchronization.
performance_targets: Packet processing latency < 2μs from NIC DMA to user-space pointer
Zero-copy buffer management (no memcpy in hot path)
Support 10+ Mpps per queue at line rate
L1 cache hit rate > 95% for ring buffer access
NUMA-local memory allocation (0 remote access)

implementation_phases.name: Environment Setup
implementation_phases.description: Configure hugepages, load VFIO driver, verify NIC compatibility

implementation_phases.name: AF_XDP Implementation
implementation_phases.description: Create UMEM, initialize fill/RX rings, bind socket to interface

implementation_phases.name: DPDK Implementation
implementation_phases.description: Initialize EAL, configure port, create mbuf pool, setup RX/TX queues

implementation_phases.name: Zero-Copy Buffer Pool
implementation_phases.description: Implement buffer allocation/deallocation with cache-line alignment

implementation_phases.name: Packet RX/TX Functions
implementation_phases.description: Implement burst receive/transmit with prefetching

implementation_phases.name: Performance Benchmarking
implementation_phases.description: Measure latency with hardware timestamps, validate < 2μs target

diagrams.title: Kernel Path vs. Bypass Path — Latency Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of kernel network stack path (interrupt → sk_buff → syscall → copy) vs. bypass path (DMA → ring buffer poll → direct pointer access). Shows latency at each stage.

diagrams.title: NIC Ring Buffer Architecture
diagrams.description: Detailed view of RX and TX ring buffers showing descriptor arrays, head/tail pointers, DMA engine interaction, and packet data buffers in RAM.

diagrams.title: DPDK vs. AF_XDP Architecture
diagrams.description: Comparison of DPDK (user-space PMD, hugepages, NIC detached from kernel) vs. AF_XDP (kernel-cooperative, XDP program, UMEM) with data flow paths.

diagrams.title: UMEM/mbuf Pool Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout showing UMEM region divided into frames, frame descriptors, cache-line boundaries, and buffer address calculation.

diagrams.title: AF_XDP Four Rings Interaction
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing fill ring (app→kernel), RX ring (kernel→app), TX ring (app→kernel), completion ring (kernel→app) with packet flow.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Buffer Pool Design
diagrams.description: Architecture of buffer pool showing free list, allocated buffers, reference counting, and cache-line padding to prevent false sharing.

diagrams.title: Buffer Pool Cache Line Layout
diagrams.description: Detailed byte-level layout of buffer pool structures showing 64-byte cache line boundaries, head/tail pointer separation, and descriptor alignment.

diagrams.title: Latency Benchmark Setup
diagrams.description: Test configuration showing packet generator, DUT (device under test), hardware timestamping points, and latency calculation methodology.

diagrams.title: Latency Distribution Histogram
diagrams.description: Example histogram showing P50, P99, P99.9 latency percentiles with explanation of why tail latency matters more than mean in HFT.

diagrams.title: Complete Stack Layers
diagrams.description: Layered view showing all milestones: M1 (kernel bypass), M2 (Ethernet/ARP), M3 (IP/UDP), M4 (TCP), M5 (optimization) with data flow between layers.

modules.name: Ethernet & ARP Layer
modules.description: Implements Ethernet frame parsing and construction, MAC address handling, and the ARP protocol for IP-to-MAC resolution. Maintains an ARP cache with timeout-based expiration. Does NOT handle IP routing or higher-layer protocols.

specs.inputs: Raw Ethernet frames from kernel bypass layer, local MAC and IP configuration
specs.outputs: Parsed Ethernet frames with payload dispatch, ARP replies, populated ARP cache
specs.abstractions: Ethernet header structure (14 bytes), MAC address as byte array, ARP packet structure (28 bytes), ARP cache as hash table with linear probing, pending packet queue for unresolved ARP
error_categories: E_ETH_FRAME_TOO_SMALL - Frame smaller than minimum 64 bytes
E_ETH_INVALID_ETHERTYPE - Unknown or unsupported ethertype
E_ARP_CACHE_MISS - IP address not in ARP cache
E_ARP_CACHE_FULL - ARP cache table exhausted
E_ARP_TIMEOUT - No response to ARP request after retries
E_ARP_MALFORMED - Invalid ARP packet structure
E_MAC_COMPARE_FAILED - MAC address comparison error (byte order issue)
specs.concurrency_model: ARP cache uses seqlock for lock-free reads with write serialization. Pending packet queue is SPSC per destination IP. Gratuitous ARP is fire-and-forget (no acknowledgment).
performance_targets: Ethernet header parse < 20 ns (L1 cache hit)
ARP cache lookup < 30 ns (hash hit, L1 cache)
ARP cache update < 50 ns (seqlock write)
Support 10 Mpps with ARP cache hit rate > 99%
ARP resolution latency < 1ms on local network

implementation_phases.name: Ethernet Header Parsing
implementation_phases.description: Implement eth_parse() with VLAN tag handling, minimum frame size check

implementation_phases.name: Ethernet Frame Construction
implementation_phases.description: Implement eth_build() with padding for minimum frame size

implementation_phases.name: ARP Packet Implementation
implementation_phases.description: Implement ARP request/reply building and parsing

implementation_phases.name: ARP Cache Design
implementation_phases.description: Implement hash table with linear probing, seqlock for thread safety

implementation_phases.name: ARP Resolution Flow
implementation_phases.description: Implement request sending, pending queue, reply handling, cache population

implementation_phases.name: Gratuitous ARP
implementation_phases.description: Implement announcement on interface up, IP conflict detection

diagrams.title: Ethernet Frame Structure — Byte Layout
diagrams.description: Complete 14-byte Ethernet header showing dst MAC (6), src MAC (6), ethertype (2), followed by payload and FCS. Includes VLAN tag extension (4 bytes) for 802.1Q.

diagrams.title: MAC Address Byte Order Confusion
diagrams.description: Diagram showing wire order vs. memory representation of MAC addresses, common pitfalls with integer interpretation, and correct byte-array comparison method.

diagrams.title: ARP Request/Reply Packet Flow
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing broadcast ARP request, unicast reply, cache population, and pending packet transmission after resolution.

diagrams.title: ARP Cache Structure
diagrams.description: Hash table architecture showing bucket array, linear probing collision resolution, entry structure (IP, MAC, state, timestamp), and TTL-based expiration.

diagrams.title: ARP Cache — Hash Table with LRU
diagrams.description: Detailed view of hash function, bucket structure, entry states (INCOMPLETE, REACHABLE, STALE, FAILED), and eviction policy.

diagrams.title: ARP Resolution State Machine
diagrams.description: State machine showing transitions: IDLE → INCOMPLETE (request sent) → REACHABLE (reply received) → STALE (timeout) → FAILED (no response).

diagrams.title: Ethernet Layer Data Flow
diagrams.description: Data flow from raw frame → eth_parse() → dispatch by ethertype (ARP/IP/unknown) → handler functions. Shows error paths and drop conditions.

modules.name: IP & UDP Layer
modules.description: Implements IPv4 header parsing and construction, IP fragmentation/reassembly, UDP datagram processing, ICMP echo (ping), and basic routing table lookup. Does NOT implement TCP—that's M4.

specs.inputs: IP packets from Ethernet layer (ethertype 0x0800), UDP send requests from application
specs.outputs: Parsed IP packets dispatched to UDP/ICMP handlers, IP packets for transmission, ICMP echo replies, routing decisions
specs.abstractions: IPv4 header (20-60 bytes), IP fragmentation bitmap, UDP socket table (port → socket), ICMP message types, routing table with longest-prefix-match
error_categories: E_IP_CHECKSUM_FAILED - IP header checksum verification failed
E_IP_VERSION_MISMATCH - Not IPv4 (version != 4)
E_IP_HEADER_TRUNCATED - Header extends beyond packet
E_IP_FRAGMENT_LOST - Fragment reassembly timeout or incomplete
E_UDP_CHECKSUM_FAILED - UDP checksum verification failed
E_UDP_PORT_UNREACHABLE - No socket bound to destination port
E_ICMP_UNHANDLED - Unhandled ICMP message type
E_ROUTE_NOT_FOUND - No matching route in routing table
specs.concurrency_model: Routing table is read-only after initialization (lock-free reads). Fragment reassembly table uses per-entry locks. UDP socket table uses atomic port allocation. ICMP is stateless (no locking).
performance_targets: IP header parse < 10 ns (L1 cache hit)
IP checksum (20 bytes) < 15 ns
UDP header parse < 5 ns
Routing table lookup < 50 ns (longest prefix match)
Fragment reassembly < 500 ns per fragment
ICMP echo reply generation < 50 ns

implementation_phases.name: IPv4 Header Parsing
implementation_phases.description: Implement ip_parse() with field extraction, version/IHL validation, checksum verification

implementation_phases.name: IP Checksum Calculation
implementation_phases.description: Implement optimized one's complement checksum with 32-bit accumulator and folding

implementation_phases.name: IP Fragmentation Handling
implementation_phases.description: Implement fragment detection, reassembly buffer, bitmap tracking, timeout handling

implementation_phases.name: UDP Socket Implementation
implementation_phases.description: Implement socket table, bind(), recv_callback, send(), checksum with pseudo-header

implementation_phases.name: ICMP Echo Responder
implementation_phases.description: Implement ICMP echo request handling, reply generation, checksum

implementation_phases.name: Routing Table
implementation_phases.description: Implement route_lookup() with longest prefix match, route_add() for static routes

diagrams.title: IPv4 Header — Complete Byte Layout
diagrams.description: 20-byte minimum header showing ver+IHL, TOS, total_len, ID, flags+fragoff, TTL, protocol, checksum, src_addr, dst_addr with bit-level field breakdown.

diagrams.title: IP Fragmentation and Reassembly
diagrams.description: Diagram showing original packet split into fragments, fragment offset calculation, MF flag, reassembly buffer with bitmap, and timeout handling.

diagrams.title: UDP Datagram Processing Pipeline
diagrams.description: Data flow from IP payload → UDP parse → port lookup → socket delivery → application callback. Shows checksum verification branch.

diagrams.title: IP/UDP/TCP Checksum Algorithm
diagrams.description: One's complement sum algorithm showing 16-bit word accumulation, carry folding, and final complement. Includes pseudo-header construction.

diagrams.title: ICMP Message Types and Layout
diagrams.description: ICMP header structure and message types: echo request/reply (8/0), destination unreachable (3), time exceeded (11). Shows data portion for each.

diagrams.title: IP Routing Table Lookup
diagrams.description: Longest prefix match algorithm showing network/mask comparison, metric tie-breaking, and route selection for default gateway vs. specific routes.

diagrams.title: Complete IP Processing Pipeline
diagrams.description: End-to-end flow from Ethernet → IP parse → checksum → fragmentation check → protocol dispatch → UDP/ICMP/TCP handler.

modules.name: TCP Implementation
modules.description: Implements the complete TCP state machine (11 states), three-way handshake, connection teardown, sequence number management, sliding window flow control, retransmission with RTO calculation, and congestion control (Reno/CUBIC). Supports 10K+ concurrent connections.

specs.inputs: TCP segments from IP layer (protocol 6), application connect/send/recv/close calls
specs.outputs: TCP segments for transmission, connection state transitions, data delivery to application, connection notifications
specs.abstractions: TCP connection control block (64+ bytes), 4-tuple connection hash table, retransmission queue, receive buffer with sequence tracking, congestion window state machine, timer wheel for timeouts
error_categories: E_TCP_CHECKSUM_FAILED - TCP checksum verification failed
E_TCP_STATE_INVALID - Segment received in unexpected state
E_TCP_SEQ_OUTSIDE_WINDOW - Sequence number outside receive window
E_TCP_CONN_TABLE_FULL - Maximum connections reached (10K)
E_TCP_RETRANS_EXHAUSTED - Maximum retransmissions exceeded
E_TCP_RST_RECEIVED - Connection reset by peer
E_TCP_SYN_FLOOD - SYN flood detected,启用 SYN cookies
E_TCP_TIME_WAIT_OVERFLOW - Too many connections in TIME_WAIT
specs.concurrency_model: Per-connection spinlocks for state modification. Lock-free connection lookup (RCU-style). Timer wheel with per-slot locks. Congestion control state is per-connection (no shared state). 10K connections requires careful memory layout.
performance_targets: TCP header parse < 10 ns
Connection lookup < 30 ns (hash hit)
State transition < 5 ns
ACK processing < 50 ns
Retransmit timer update < 20 ns
Support 10K concurrent connections with < 1MB connection table
Handle 1M+ segments per second per connection

implementation_phases.name: TCP Header Parsing
implementation_phases.description: Implement tcp_parse() with option handling, checksum verification with pseudo-header

implementation_phases.name: Connection State Machine
implementation_phases.description: Implement 11-state machine with transitions for SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK/FIN/RST

implementation_phases.name: Three-Way Handshake
implementation_phases.description: Implement connect(), SYN generation, SYN-ACK handling, ISN selection, SYN cookies

implementation_phases.name: Connection Teardown
implementation_phases.description: Implement close(), FIN handling, TIME_WAIT state, simultaneous close

implementation_phases.name: Sliding Window Flow Control
implementation_phases.description: Implement send/receive windows, window update tracking, zero-window probes

implementation_phases.name: Retransmission Timers
implementation_phases.description: Implement Jacobson/Karels RTO calculation, retransmission queue, fast retransmit

implementation_phases.name: Congestion Control (Reno/CUBIC)
implementation_phases.description: Implement slow start, congestion avoidance, fast recovery, CUBIC window function

implementation_phases.name: Connection Table Scaling
implementation_phases.description: Implement 10K connection hash table, per-connection locks, timer wheel

diagrams.title: TCP Header — Complete Byte Layout
diagrams.description: 20-byte minimum header with all fields: ports, seq, ack, data_offset, flags, window, checksum, urgent_ptr. Shows option layout for MSS, wscale, timestamps, SACK.

diagrams.title: TCP State Machine — Complete
diagrams.description: 11-state machine showing all transitions: CLOSED → LISTEN → SYN_SENT → SYN_RECEIVED → ESTABLISHED → FIN_WAIT_1 → FIN_WAIT_2 → TIME_WAIT and parallel path through CLOSE_WAIT → LAST_ACK.

diagrams.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
diagrams.description: Packet sequence showing client SYN (seq=ISN_c) → server SYN-ACK (seq=ISN_s, ack=ISN_c+1) → client ACK (seq=ISN_c+1, ack=ISN_s+1). State transitions annotated.

diagrams.title: TCP Connection Teardown
diagrams.description: FIN/RST paths showing active close (FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK → TIME_WAIT) and RST abort. Includes simultaneous close case.

diagrams.title: TCP Sliding Window Mechanics
diagrams.description: Visual showing send window (SND_UNA to SND_UNA+SND_WND), in-flight data, usable window calculation, and window sliding as ACKs arrive.

diagrams.title: TCP Retransmission Timer
diagrams.description: RTO calculation flow: RTT sample → Jacobson/Karels (RTTVAR, SRTT) → RTO = SRTT + 4*RTTVAR. Shows exponential backoff on timeout.

diagrams.title: TCP Reno Congestion Control — AIMD
diagrams.description: Graph showing cwnd over time: slow start (exponential), congestion avoidance (linear), loss detection (cwnd halved), fast recovery. AIMD behavior illustrated.

diagrams.title: TCP Connection Table Design
diagrams.description: Hash table architecture for 10K connections: hash function on 4-tuple, bucket chains, connection control block layout, cache-line alignment.

diagrams.title: SYN Cookies — SYN Flood Defense
diagrams.description: SYN cookie generation (encode MSS + hash of 4-tuple) and validation flow. Shows stateless operation during flood vs. normal connection tracking.

diagrams.title: TCP Input Processing State Machine
diagrams.description: Decision tree for incoming segment: lookup connection → check state → validate seq → process flags → update state → send response.

diagrams.title: Timer Wheel Architecture
diagrams.description: Timing wheel with 4096 slots, 10ms tick, showing timer insertion, tick processing, and O(1) add/remove operations for 10K connections.

modules.name: Optimization & Production Hardening
modules.description: Optimizes the stack for ultra-low latency through NUMA-aware allocation, lock-free data paths, cache-optimized structures, and batch processing. Adds production features: statistics collection, packet capture for debugging, graceful degradation under load, and deployment documentation.

specs.inputs: Complete TCP/IP stack from M1-M4, system configuration (NUMA topology, CPU affinity)
specs.outputs: Sub-5μs packet-to-application latency, Prometheus-compatible metrics, packet capture files, graceful degradation under overload
specs.abstractions: NUMA-local memory pools, per-core statistics counters, latency histogram (power-of-2 buckets), capture ring buffer, load monitor with backpressure, adaptive batch processor
error_categories: E_NUMA_REMOTE_ACCESS - Memory access across NUMA nodes (>100ns penalty)
E_CACHE_MISS_HOT_PATH - Cache miss in critical data path
E_LOCK_CONTENTION - Lock acquired in hot path (unacceptable)
E_BATCH_TIMEOUT - Batch processing delayed waiting for full batch
E_CAPTURE_RING_FULL - Packet capture ring buffer overflow
E_LOAD_SHEDDING - Packets dropped due to overload
E_CONFIG_DRIFT - Production configuration differs from expected
specs.concurrency_model: Zero locks in data path. Per-core statistics (aggregated on demand). Lock-free capture ring buffer (SPSC). RCU-style connection table reads. Atomic-free latency recording (per-core histogram).
performance_targets: End-to-end latency < 5μs (P99) for packet-to-application
L1 cache hit rate > 95% for all hot-path data
Zero lock acquisitions in packet processing path
NUMA-local memory allocation (0% remote access)
Statistics collection overhead < 10 ns per packet
Support graceful degradation at 150% of rated capacity

implementation_phases.name: NUMA-Aware Initialization
implementation_phases.description: Detect NIC NUMA node, allocate memory on correct node, pin processing thread

implementation_phases.name: Lock-Free Data Path Verification
implementation_phases.description: Audit all hot paths, convert remaining locks to lock-free structures

implementation_phases.name: Cache-Optimized Structure Layout
implementation_phases.description: Reorganize connection structures for cache-line alignment, separate hot/cold fields

implementation_phases.name: Per-Core Statistics
implementation_phases.description: Implement per-core counters, latency histogram, aggregation thread

implementation_phases.name: Batch Processing with Adaptive Sizing
implementation_phases.description: Implement batch processor with timeout, adaptive batch size based on load

implementation_phases.name: Packet Capture System
implementation_phases.description: Implement lock-free capture ring, sampling, PCAP export

implementation_phases.name: Graceful Degradation
implementation_phases.description: Implement load monitor, priority-based packet dropping, backpressure API

implementation_phases.name: Deployment Documentation
implementation_phases.description: Create deployment guide, BIOS settings, kernel parameters, health checks, troubleshooting

diagrams.title: NUMA Topology for Packet Processing
diagrams.description: Dual-socket server showing NUMA nodes, memory controllers, PCIe slots for NICs, QPI/UPI interconnect, and optimal placement for single-socket vs. dual-socket configurations.

diagrams.title: Packet Processing Cache Locality
diagrams.description: Cache hierarchy (L1/L2/L3) with packet processing data flow, showing which data structures should be in which cache level for optimal latency.

diagrams.title: Lock-Free Ring Buffer for Packet Queues
diagrams.description: SPSC ring buffer showing head/tail separation (different cache lines), memory barriers, producer/consumer pseudocode, and overflow handling.

diagrams.title: Statistics Collection — Lock-Free Counters
diagrams.description: Per-core statistics array showing cache-line separation, update path (no atomics), aggregation thread reading all cores, and Prometheus export format.

diagrams.title: Batch Size vs. Latency Trade-off
diagrams.description: Graph showing latency vs. batch size: small batches have low latency but high overhead, large batches amortize overhead but increase wait time. Shows optimal operating point.

diagrams.title: In-Stack Packet Capture for Debugging
diagrams.description: Capture architecture showing lock-free ring buffer, sampling filter, PCAP writer thread, and non-blocking drop when ring is full.

diagrams.title: Production Deployment Architecture
diagrams.description: Production setup showing isolated CPU cores, hugepage memory, NIC on correct NUMA node, management interface, and monitoring/health check endpoints.

diagrams.title: Zero-Copy Data Path — Buffer Lifetime
diagrams.description: Buffer lifecycle from allocation (mempool) → NIC DMA → parse (no copy) → application delivery → free back to pool. Shows zero-copy preservation throughout.

diagrams.title: Latency Budget Breakdown
diagrams.description: Pie chart or bar graph showing where 5μs budget is spent: NIC DMA, ring poll, Ethernet parse, IP parse, TCP parse, connection lookup, processing, delivery, and margin.

diagrams.title: Load Shedding State Machine
diagrams.description: Load monitor states (NORMAL → HIGH → CRITICAL) with transitions based on queue depth and processing rate. Shows packet acceptance policy per state.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Kernel Path vs. Bypass Path — Latency Comparison
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Side-by-side comparison of kernel network stack path (interrupt → sk_buff → syscall → copy) vs. bypass path (DMA → ring buffer poll → direct pointer access). Shows latency at each stage.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: NIC Ring Buffer Architecture
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Detailed view of RX and TX ring buffers showing descriptor arrays, head/tail pointers, DMA engine interaction, and packet data buffers in RAM.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: DPDK vs. AF_XDP Architecture
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Comparison of DPDK (user-space PMD, hugepages, NIC detached from kernel) vs. AF_XDP (kernel-cooperative, XDP program, UMEM) with data flow paths.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: UMEM/mbuf Pool Memory Layout
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Memory layout showing UMEM region divided into frames, frame descriptors, cache-line boundaries, and buffer address calculation.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: AF_XDP Four Rings Interaction
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Sequence diagram showing fill ring (app→kernel), RX ring (kernel→app), TX ring (app→kernel), completion ring (kernel→app) with packet flow.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Zero-Copy Buffer Pool Design
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Architecture of buffer pool showing free list, allocated buffers, reference counting, and cache-line padding to prevent false sharing.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Buffer Pool Cache Line Layout
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Detailed byte-level layout of buffer pool structures showing 64-byte cache line boundaries, head/tail pointer separation, and descriptor alignment.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Latency Benchmark Setup
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Test configuration showing packet generator, DUT (device under test), hardware timestamping points, and latency calculation methodology.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Latency Distribution Histogram
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Example histogram showing P50, P99, P99.9 latency percentiles with explanation of why tail latency matters more than mean in HFT.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Complete Stack Layers
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Layered view showing all milestones: M1 (kernel bypass), M2 (Ethernet/ARP), M3 (IP/UDP), M4 (TCP), M5 (optimization) with data flow between layers.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Ethernet Frame Structure — Byte Layout
